explanation.text: The snake warns that the charm will stop working if Niel reveals his secret. Niel answers, “I won’t tell anyone,” which shows that reveal means to tell or make something known.
Niel clasps the charm just after hearing the mice and realizing that he understands their squeaking. This shows that the charm has given him the promised ability to understand animals.
Niel repeatedly laughs at the animals’ comments but refuses to explain why. Because his behavior makes his wife increasingly suspicious and angry, it creates the story’s main conflict between them.

Paragraph 3 states that wearing the charm lets Niel understand everything animals say. Because it also explains that revealing the secret ends that power, this paragraph gives both the ability and its limit.
Niel has already promised once to keep the secret and then told his wife. The snake’s challenge, “We’ll see how long you last,” therefore shows that he expects Niel to reveal the new secret too.
The charm seems like a wonderful reward, but Niel’s unexplained laughter nearly drives his wife away, and telling her makes the charm fail. Therefore, the reward creates more trouble than enjoyment.

Dave explains that paleontologists and fossil hunters may search for weeks and rarely find a whole skeleton. Because the dig focuses on locating and carefully uncovering dinosaur fossils, a paleontologist studies ancient animal bones.
Dave notices that Benny may be tired of the slow search and offers him a different job with the crew. This shows that Dave is concerned Benny may be unhappy or discouraged.
Benny decides, “I’ll keep looking,” even though another job might be more fun. He then carefully picks, chips, brushes, and sweeps in the growing heat, which shows that he continues to work hard.
Before paragraph 12, Benny is bored, hot, and becoming drowsy. He then discovers the tooth and becomes surprised and excited. This shows why paragraph 12 marks the change in his feelings.
Benny expected a dig like the pictures, but Dave forbids shovels because they could break fossils. Instead, the crew uses ice picks and paintbrushes to remove dirt slowly and carefully.

Each young animal practices through play for skills it will need as an adult, such as escaping predators, hunting, communicating, or keeping its footing. This shows that they are preparing for later life.
The fawn “leap[s] and frolic[s]” in a twisting, twirling dance while strengthening its legs. This shows that frolic means to run or move around playfully.
The cub slinks on crouched legs before it pounces on another cub. This shows that “stalks” means it follows or approaches silently so it can surprise its target.

The fawn practices escaping predators, while the lion cubs practice stalking, pouncing, and hunting. These examples support the main idea because they show what young animals must learn to survive as adults.

Wolves must communicate before they can hunt and protect one another as a pack. The pups practice gestures such as bowing, wagging, and holding their mouths open, which shows that they first learn to use signals.
When one youngster becomes too rough, the other young animals communicate that they are unhappy. This shows that the animals set limits on one another’s behavior so play remains safe for everyone.

The article’s many examples show play building the strength, hunting, communication, and safety skills wild animals need. Therefore, the statement that play is important to their survival best covers the entire article.
